SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs comprehensive comparisons against a wide set of baselines on the NYT distant-supervision dataset and reports concrete gains (best F1, ~3% over CoType), and it includes sensitivity analysis of the bias parameter α and shows its effect on reducing single (unpaired) entities. It also compares variant models (e.g., LSTM-LSTM vs LSTM-LSTM-Bias and LSTM-CRF), indicating some ablation. However, evaluation is limited to a single distant-supervision corpus, the method cannot handle overlapping relations (acknowledged), and code availability is not mentioned so reproduction is only partially supported despite reported hyperparameters. | B(4): The experiments compare against a wide set of baselines on the NYT distant-supervision dataset and report a clear F1 improvement, supporting the main claim; the paper also provides hyperparameters and studies the biased objective (α) including its effect on single (unpaired) entities. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and the key limitation (no overlapping relations / single-triplet per entity) is explicitly discussed. However, evaluation is limited to a single dataset, code is not mentioned, and finer-grained ablations (beyond the bias sensitivity) are not clearly described, so the study is solid but has minor gaps for full reproducibility and breadth.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs comprehensive comparisons against a wide set of baselines on the NYT distant-supervision dataset and reports concrete gains (best F1, ~3% over CoType), and it includes sensitivity analysis of the bias parameter α and shows its effect on reducing single (unpaired) entities. It also compares variant models (e.g., LSTM-LSTM vs LSTM-LSTM-Bias and LSTM-CRF), indicating some ablation. However, evaluation is limited to a single distant-supervision corpus, the method cannot handle overlapping relations (acknowledged), and code availability is not mentioned so reproduction is only partially supported despite reported hyperparameters.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments compare against a wide set of baselines on the NYT distant-supervision dataset and report a clear F1 improvement, supporting the main claim; the paper also provides hyperparameters and studies the biased objective (α) including its effect on single (unpaired) entities. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and the key limitation (no overlapping relations / single-triplet per entity) is explicitly discussed. However, evaluation is limited to a single dataset, code is not mentioned, and finer-grained ablations (beyond the bias sensitivity) are not clearly described, so the study is solid but has minor gaps for full reproducibility and breadth.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses joint entity and relation extraction — a central NLP problem — and proposes a novel token-level tagging formulation with an end-to-end BiLSTM/LSTM model, which is directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences. It uses community-expected methods and evaluation (NYT distant-supervision dataset, precision/recall/F1, comparisons to established baselines like CoType and LSTM-CRF) and reports hyperparameters and analysis (bias parameter α). Although it does not handle overlapping relations (entities in multiple triplets), the problem formulation, methodology, and evaluation are appropriately positioned for a top conference. | B(5): This paper addresses a core NLP task (joint entity and relation extraction) and proposes a novel, broadly relevant formulation (token-level tagging encoding BIES, relation type, and role) and an end-to-end BiLSTM/LSTM model with a biased objective—work that fits well in top NLP/ML venues. Evaluation uses a standard distant-supervision NYT dataset, compares to appropriate baselines (CoType, MultiR, etc.), and reports precision/recall/F1, matching community expectations. Although it notes limitations (single-triplet per entity, predefined relation set), the problem, methods, and positioning are clearly aimed at the target venue’s audience and standards.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses joint entity and relation extraction — a central NLP problem — and proposes a novel token-level tagging formulation with an end-to-end BiLSTM/LSTM model, which is directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences. It uses community-expected methods and evaluation (NYT distant-supervision dataset, precision/recall/F1, comparisons to established baselines like CoType and LSTM-CRF) and reports hyperparameters and analysis (bias parameter α). Although it does not handle overlapping relations (entities in multiple triplets), the problem formulation, methodology, and evaluation are appropriately positioned for a top conference.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP task (joint entity and relation extraction) and proposes a novel, broadly relevant formulation (token-level tagging encoding BIES, relation type, and role) and an end-to-end BiLSTM/LSTM model with a biased objective—work that fits well in top NLP/ML venues. Evaluation uses a standard distant-supervision NYT dataset, compares to appropriate baselines (CoType, MultiR, etc.), and reports precision/recall/F1, matching community expectations. Although it notes limitations (single-triplet per entity, predefined relation set), the problem, methods, and positioning are clearly aimed at the target venue’s audience and standards.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important problem (joint entity and relation extraction) and introduces a novel token-level tagging scheme plus an end-to-end BiLSTM-LSTM-Bias model that empirically outperforms many baselines on the NYT distant-supervision dataset (reported ~3% F1 gain over CoType). Its biased objective that reduces single unpaired entities and lets practitioners trade off precision/recall is practically useful. However, the approach is constrained by assuming entities participate in a single triplet and cannot handle overlapping relations, and results are shown only on one distant-supervision dataset, so the contribution is helpful and likely to be adopted but not yet transformative. | B(3): The paper tackles the important task of joint entity and relation extraction and proposes a novel token-level tagging scheme plus an end-to-end BiLSTM-LSTM model with a biased objective, which empirically yields a meaningful improvement (about 3% F1 over CoType) on the NYT distant-supervision dataset. The conversion to sequence tagging is a simple, practical idea that could influence how researchers structure joint extraction models and the biased loss provides a useful mechanism to trade precision and recall. However, the approach is limited by its inability to handle overlapping relations and reliance on a predefined relation set and a single dataset, which constrain immediate broad applicability and limit transformative impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem (joint entity and relation extraction) and introduces a novel token-level tagging scheme plus an end-to-end BiLSTM-LSTM-Bias model that empirically outperforms many baselines on the NYT distant-supervision dataset (reported ~3% F1 gain over CoType). Its biased objective that reduces single unpaired entities and lets practitioners trade off precision/recall is practically useful. However, the approach is constrained by assuming entities participate in a single triplet and cannot handle overlapping relations, and results are shown only on one distant-supervision dataset, so the contribution is helpful and likely to be adopted but not yet transformative.

The conversion to sequence tagging is a simple, practical idea that could influence how researchers structure joint extraction models and the biased loss provides a useful mechanism to trade precision and recall. However, the approach is limited by its inability to handle overlapping relations and reliance on a predefined relation set and a single dataset, which constrain immediate broad applicability and limit transformative impact.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and presents its main contributions early (the novel BIES+relation-role tagging scheme and the LSTM-LSTM-Bias model) with a clear method->experiments->conclusion flow. Notation and methodological steps are described consistently and hyperparameters are reported; experimental comparisons on the NYT dataset (precision/recall/F1) make the results easy to interpret. Writing quality is good and the paper explicitly states limitations (e.g., single-triplet assumption), so overall clarity is high with only minor presentation issues. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear progression from motivation to method (novel tagging scheme) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (tagging scheme, BiLSTM+LSTM decoder with a biased objective) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and well-defined in the summary (tags encode BIES position, relation type, and role), and experimental presentation is effective—results (3% F1 gain on NYT, α=10 choice) and hyperparameters are reported. Writing quality is described as good with adequate related work coverage; the only issues implied are minor presentation choices (e.g., handling of overlapping relations is deferred) rather than clarity problems.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad set of relevant baselines (DS-logistic, LINE, FCM, DS-Joint, MultiR, CoType, LSTM-CRF, LSTM-LSTM) on the same NYT dataset using standard precision/recall/F1 metrics and reports improvements (e.g., +3% F1 over CoType). It also discusses limitations and related work coverage is described as adequate. Minor omissions of newer or alternative recent SOTA approaches are possible, but overall the comparisons are good and generally fair. | B(4): The paper compares against a broad set of baselines (DS-logistic, LINE, FCM, DS-Joint, MultiR, CoType, LSTM-CRF, LSTM-LSTM) on the NYT distant-supervision dataset using standard Precision/Recall/F1 metrics and reports a clear improvement (≈3% F1 over CoType). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and related work coverage is described as adequate, with limitations acknowledged. However, the summary does not indicate whether all recent state-of-the-art alternatives or any methods that might perform comparably/better were fully discussed, so there may be minor omissions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of relevant baselines (DS-logistic, LINE, FCM, DS-Joint, MultiR, CoType, LSTM-CRF, LSTM-LSTM) on the same NYT dataset using standard precision/recall/F1 metrics and reports improvements (e.g., +3% F1 over CoType). It also discusses limitations and related work coverage is described as adequate. Minor omissions of newer or alternative recent SOTA approaches are possible, but overall the comparisons are good and generally fair.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of baselines (DS-logistic, LINE, FCM, DS-Joint, MultiR, CoType, LSTM-CRF, LSTM-LSTM) on the NYT distant-supervision dataset using standard Precision/Recall/F1 metrics and reports a clear improvement (≈3% F1 over CoType). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics) and related work coverage is described as adequate, with limitations acknowledged. ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a genuinely new tagging scheme that encodes token position (BIES), relation type, and relation role to convert joint entity-and-relation extraction into a sequence tagging problem, which is a clear conceptual novelty. It also proposes an end-to-end BiLSTM encoder + LSTM decoder model with a biased objective (LSTM-LSTM-Bias) tailored to that scheme and explicitly differentiates itself from prior joint and pipelined methods in experiments. However, the approach is still an application/combination of existing sequence models and is limited by the single-triplet-per-entity assumption and reliance on a predefined relation set, so the contribution is notable but not a radical departure from prior work. | B(4): The paper introduces a clearly new formulation by converting joint entity and relation extraction into a token-level tagging problem via a novel tag that encodes BIES position, relation type, and relation role, and implements an end-to-end BiLSTM encoder + LSTM decoder with a biased objective (LSTM-LSTM-Bias). This differentiates it from prior pipelined or standard joint methods and yields measurable gains on NYT, showing a non-trivial methodological extension. The approach is limited (handles only single-triplet membership per entity and relies on a predefined relation set), so while the idea is notable and would inform experts, it is not a radical breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a genuinely new tagging scheme that encodes token position (BIES), relation type, and relation role to convert joint entity-and-relation extraction into a sequence tagging problem, which is a clear conceptual novelty. It also proposes an end-to-end BiLSTM encoder + LSTM decoder model with a biased objective (LSTM-LSTM-Bias) tailored to that scheme and explicitly differentiates itself from prior joint and pipelined methods in experiments. However, the approach is still an application/combination of existing sequence models and is limited by the single-triplet-per-entity assumption and reliance on a predefined relation set, so the contribution is notable but not a radical departure from prior work.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper introduces a clearly new formulation by converting joint entity and relation extraction into a token-level tagging problem via a novel tag that encodes BIES position, relation type, and relation role, and implements an end-to-end BiLSTM encoder + LSTM decoder with a biased objective (LSTM-LSTM-Bias). This differentiates it from prior pipelined or standard joint methods and yields measurable gains on NYT, showing a non-trivial methodological extension. The approach is limited (handles only single-triplet membership per entity and relies on a predefined relation set), so while the idea is notable and would inform experts, it is not a radical breakthrough.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's primary claims (a novel BIES+relation+role tagging scheme and an end-to-end BiLSTM/LSTM model with a biased objective) are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence on the NYT distant-supervision dataset, including a reported ~3% F1 improvement over CoType and analysis showing the bias reduces single (unpaired) entities and allows precision/recall trade-offs via α. Mathematical content appears limited and consistent with the described architecture, and comparisons include many relevant baselines (CoType, MultiR, LSTM-CRF, etc.). Minor concerns are that evaluation is reported only on one distant-supervision dataset (with no mention of statistical significance or multiple runs) and the approach explicitly ignores overlapping relations and assumes entities belong to a single triplet and a fixed relation set — limitations that constrain generality but do not invalidate the reported results. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence on the NYT distant-supervision dataset (reporting LSTM-LSTM-Bias achieves the best F1 and a ~3% improvement over CoType) and by a concrete model (novel tagging scheme + BiLSTM encoder + LSTM decoder with a biased objective). The technical approach (token-level tags encoding BIES, relation type and role, and the biased loss with tunable α) is plausible and internally consistent, and comparisons include a reasonable set of baselines. However, the evaluation is limited to a single noisy dataset, no statistical significance testing is reported, and the method’s restrictive assumption that entities belong to a single triplet (no overlapping relations) is a notable limitation that weakens broad applicability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's primary claims (a novel BIES+relation+role tagging scheme and an end-to-end BiLSTM/LSTM model with a biased objective) are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence on the NYT distant-supervision dataset, including a reported ~3% F1 improvement over CoType and analysis showing the bias reduces single (unpaired) entities and allows precision/recall trade-offs via α. Mathematical content appears limited and consistent with the described architecture, and comparisons include many relevant baselines (CoType, MultiR, LSTM-CRF, etc.). Minor concerns are that evaluation is reported only on one distant-supervision dataset (with no mention of statistical significance or multiple runs) and the approach explicitly ignores overlapping relations and assumes entities belong to a single triplet and a fixed relation set — limitations that constrain generality but do not invalidate the reported results.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence on the NYT distant-supervision dataset (reporting LSTM-LSTM-Bias achieves the best F1 and a ~3% improvement over CoType) and by a concrete model (novel tagging scheme + BiLSTM encoder + LSTM decoder with a biased objective). The technical approach (token-level tags encoding BIES, relation type and role, and the biased loss with tunable α) is plausible and internally consistent, and comparisons include a reasonable set of baselines. However, the evaluation is limited to a single noisy dataset, no statistical significance testing is reported, and the method’s restrictive assumption that entities belong to a single triplet (no overlapping relations) is a notable limitation that weakens broad applicability.

main_claims: Proposes a novel tagging scheme that converts joint entity and relation extraction into a sequence tagging problem by assigning each token a label encoding position (BIES), relation type, and relation role (1/2).
Presents an end-to-end model (BiLSTM encoder + LSTM decoder) with a biased objective function (LSTM-LSTM-Bias) to jointly extract entities and relations directly from the tagged sequence.

Shows that tagging-based methods outperform most existing pipelined and joint methods on the NYT distant-supervision dataset, and that LSTM-LSTM-Bias achieves the best F1 score (reported a 3% F1 improvement over CoType).
Demonstrates that the biased objective reduces the ratio of single (unpaired) entities and allows balancing precision and recall via the bias parameter α (best at α=10).
_summary.methodology: Convert joint extraction to token-level tagging where each tag encodes entity position (BIES), relation type (from a predefined set), and relation role (1 or 2); then use a BiLSTM encoding layer and an LSTM decoding layer trained with a biased objective (bias parameter α, default 10) to predict tags and combine tokens with matching relation-type/roles into triplets.
datasets: NYT (distant supervision dataset from Ren et al., 2017)
limitations: Only considers the situation where an entity belongs to a single triplet and does not handle overlapping relations (left for future work).
Relies on a predefined relation set (relation labels are taken from this set and may not appear as words in the sentence).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - joint entity and relation extraction
